title: Workbook — Holy Orders
html:
Student Workbook

For use after the lesson or in a group. Print this page or work in your notebook.

Remembering the Essentials

  1. What are the three degrees of Holy Orders? Briefly explain the function of each.
  2. What is the difference between the common priesthood of all the baptized and the ministerial priesthood?
  3. What does it mean that the priest acts in persona Christi Capitis?
  4. When did Jesus institute the priesthood and with what words?
  5. What is apostolic succession and why is it important?
  6. What are the three offices (munera) of Christ exercised by the ordained minister?

For Reflection and Writing

Answer each question honestly. There are no «correct» answers to these personal questions.

1. Which priest has marked your life? Write his name and three qualities you admire in him.

 

 

 

2. How do you live your baptismal priesthood in daily life? Give concrete examples.

 

 

 

3. The Curé of Ars said: «The priesthood is the love of the Heart of Jesus.» What does this mean to you? Do you think people today understand the greatness of the priesthood?

 

 

 

4. Have you ever thought that God might be calling you to the priesthood or the consecrated life? Why or why not?

 

 

 

5. What can you do concretely this week to support the priests of your parish?

 

 

Practical Activities

A. A Letter to a Priest

Choose a priest who has had an impact on your life (your pastor, a confessor, a chaplain). Write a short letter thanking him for three specific things. If he is alive, would you send it to him this very week? If he has passed away, write it as a prayer.

B. The Three Degrees in Drawing

Draw three simple symbols representing the bishop (crozier and miter), the priest (stole and chalice), and the deacon (dalmatic and Book of the Gospels). Next to each, write his primary function. If you can't draw, you may cut out images from the internet or simply write the names.

C. Biblical Search: The Laying on of Hands

Search the New Testament for at least three passages where the laying on of hands is mentioned to confer a ministry (hint: Acts 6:1-6; 1 Tm 4:14; 2 Tm 1:6). Read them and note: what do they have in common? Why does the Church continue to use this same gesture at ordinations?

D. Prayer for Vocations

Commit to praying each day this week for the seminarians of your diocese and for the young people God is calling to the priesthood. Holy Thursday is traditionally a special day of prayer for priests. If you don't know how many seminarians are in your diocese, look it up online. You might also want to look up the name of your diocesan vocations director and include him in your prayers.

E. Reflection: The Hands of a Priest

A priest's hands are consecrated to touch the Body of Christ. Think about what those hands do each day: they bless, they absolve, they anoint the sick, they lift up the Eucharist. Write a short prayer specifically for the hands of a priest you know — that they may always be instruments of grace and never of harm.

Scripture Verse to Memorize

«You did not choose me, but I chose you.
And I appointed you to go and bear fruit,
fruit that will last.»

— John 15:16

Prayer for This Week

Lord Jesus, Supreme and Eternal High Priest,
I thank you for the gift of the priesthood.
Bless the bishops, priests, and deacons
you have placed in my path.
Protect them from evil, comfort them in their trials,
and help them live their ministry with joy and fidelity.
Raise up in the Church many holy vocations
to the priesthood and the consecrated life.
And grant me the grace to live fully
my own baptismal priesthood,
offering my life each day for love.
Amen.

Print Workbook
